The apparent rarity of this species in their habitat and high levels of aggression between consexuals suggests the possibility of some sort of plasticity in their primary sex determination. In this study, the influence of social environment upon primary sex determination in <em>H. picta</em> was examined experimentally in the laboratory. Naïve juveniles were placed in three novel social environments: paired juveniles, single juveniles, and adult/juvenile pairs. Spacing behavior and the onset of external differentiation were observed during long-term experiments for each social treatment. Gonadal development was also observed. Spacing behavior of paired shrimp reflected the sexual composition of social groups; high intrapair distance (IPD) for same-sex pairs and low IPD for opposite-sex pairs. Sex determination results were not so clear. Two of the three paired juvenile replicates displayed phenotype frequencies different from those expected from a random sample of gonochoristic organisms with a 1:1 sex ratio. However, the third replicate and the combined frequencies did not show any statistical difference from the hypothetical random sample. Furthermore, single juveniles and those paired with adults expressed sex in nearly 1:1 ratios, regardless of the sex of adult conspecifics. Histological examination of juvenile shrimp confirmed that sex was determined as early as four weeks after larval metamorphosis. Therefore, social environment had no clear effect upon sexual phenotype expressed. However, single females attained puberty at a significantly greater age and larger size than did females paired with males. Hence for <em>H. picta</em>, social environment had a measurable effect on the timing of female puberty. This is the first demonstration of this phenomenon in decapod Crustacea.</p></section>                        <div class="article-metadata-panel clearfix"></div>\n<p>The phenomenon of environmental sex determination (ESD) (<a reveal-id="R7" data-open="R7" class="link link-ref link-reveal xref-bibr">Bull, 1983</a>) is well documented in many teleost fish families in the form of some sort of sequential hermaphroditism (<a reveal-id="R1" data-open="R1" class="link link-ref link-reveal xref-bibr">Atz, 1964</a>; <a reveal-id="R37" data-open="R37" class="link link-ref link-reveal xref-bibr">Smith, 1975</a>; <a reveal-id="R44" data-open="R44" class="link link-ref link-reveal xref-bibr">Yogo, 1987</a>). The best such examples in fish involve the social environment and its effect on sex (e.g., <a reveal-id="R17" data-open="R17" class="link link-ref link-reveal xref-bibr">Fricke and Fricke, 1977</a>; <a reveal-id="R26" data-open="R26" class="link link-ref link-reveal xref-bibr">Moyer and Nakazono, 1978</a>; <a reveal-id="R39" data-open="R39" class="link link-ref link-reveal xref-bibr">Warner, 1982</a>). In crustaceans, however, there appears to be little direct evidence of social control of functional sex. The best known examples occur in parasitic bopyrid isopods and protandrous hermaphrodite shrimp of the family Pandalidae. In bopyrid examples, the first individual to reach a host becomes female and may cause subsequent conspecifics to become male (<a reveal-id="R33" data-open="R33" class="link link-ref link-reveal xref-bibr">Reverberi, 1944</a>; <a reveal-id="R32" data-open="R32" class="link link-ref link-reveal xref-bibr">Reinhard, 1949</a>; <a reveal-id="R10" data-open="R10" class="link'}]

The apparent rarity of this species in their habitat and high levels of aggression between consexuals suggests the possibility of some sort of plasticity in their primary sex determination. In this study, the influence of social environment upon primary sex determination in <em>H. picta</em> was examined experimentally in the laboratory. Naïve juveniles were placed in three novel social environments: paired juveniles, single juveniles, and adult/juvenile pairs. Spacing behavior and the onset of external differentiation were observed during long-term experiments for each social treatment. Gonadal development was also observed. Spacing behavior of paired shrimp reflected the sexual composition of social groups; high intrapair distance (IPD) for same-sex pairs and low IPD for opposite-sex pairs. Sex determination results were not so clear. Two of the three paired juvenile replicates displayed phenotype frequencies different from those expected from a random sample of gonochoristic organisms with a 1:1 sex ratio. However, the third replicate and the combined frequencies did not show any statistical difference from the hypothetical random sample. Furthermore, single juveniles and those paired with adults expressed sex in nearly 1:1 ratios, regardless of the sex of adult conspecifics. Histological examination of juvenile shrimp confirmed that sex was determined as early as four weeks after larval metamorphosis. Therefore, social environment had no clear effect upon sexual phenotype expressed. However, single females attained puberty at a significantly greater age and larger size than did females paired with males. Hence for <em>H. picta</em>, social environment had a measurable effect on the timing of female puberty. This is the first demonstration of this phenomenon in decapod Crustacea.</p></section>                        <div class="article-metadata-panel clearfix"></div>\n<p>The phenomenon of environmental sex determination (ESD) (<a reveal-id="R7" data-open="R7" class="link link-ref link-reveal xref-bibr">Bull, 1983</a>) is well documented in many teleost fish families in the form of some sort of sequential hermaphroditism (<a reveal-id="R1" data-open="R1" class="link link-ref link-reveal xref-bibr">Atz, 1964</a>; <a reveal-id="R37" data-open="R37" class="link link-ref link-reveal xref-bibr">Smith, 1975</a>; <a reveal-id="R44" data-open="R44" class="link link-ref link-reveal xref-bibr">Yogo, 1987</a>). The best such examples in fish involve the social environment and its effect on sex (e.g., <a reveal-id="R17" data-open="R17" class="link link-ref link-reveal xref-bibr">Fricke and Fricke, 1977</a>; <a reveal-id="R26" data-open="R26" class="link link-ref link-reveal xref-bibr">Moyer and Nakazono, 1978</a>; <a reveal-id="R39" data-open="R39" class="link link-ref link-reveal xref-bibr">Warner, 1982</a>). In crustaceans, however, there appears to be little direct evidence of social control of functional sex. The best known examples occur in parasitic bopyrid isopods and protandrous hermaphrodite shrimp of the family Pandalidae. In bopyrid examples, the first individual to reach a host becomes female and may cause subsequent conspecifics to become male (<a reveal-id="R33" data-open="R33" class="link link-ref link-reveal xref-bibr">Reverberi, 1944</a>; <a reveal-id="R32" data-open="R32" class="link link-ref link-reveal xref-bibr">Reinhard, 1949</a>; <a reveal-id="R10" data-open="R10" class="link\nAddress: http://web.archive.org/web/20180603235849/https://academic.oup.com/jcb/article/22/4/750/2679743\nYou previously visited this page 28 seconds ago.\nViewport position: Showing page 14 of 45.\n=======================\nThe search string \'sea star\' was not found on this page.'}]

[{'type': 'text', 'text': 'Observation:\nAddress: http://web.archive.org/web/20180603235849/https://academic.oup.com/jcb/article/22/4/750/2679743\nYou previously visited this page 37 seconds ago.\nViewport position: Showing page 15 of 45.\n=======================\nlink-ref link-reveal xref-bibr">Charniaux-Cotton <em>et al.</em>, 1983</a>). In pandalid shrimp, the timing of male-female sex change is thought to be influenced by the demographics of their populations (<a reveal-id="R11" data-open="R11" class="link link-ref link-reveal xref-bibr">Charnov, 1982</a>). However, the mechanism of such a system is in doubt (<a reveal-id="R5" data-open="R5" class="link link-ref link-reveal xref-bibr">Bergström, 1997</a>).</p><p>Examples of hermaphroditism in the malacostracan Crustacea are relatively uncommon and usually are in the form of protandrous sex change. Hermaphroditism is known from many species in a few subgroups of isopods and tanaidaceans (<a reveal-id="R9" data-open="R9" class="link link-ref link-reveal xref-bibr">Charniaux-Cotton, 1975</a>; <a reveal-id="R29" data-open="R29" class="link link-ref link-reveal xref-bibr">Policansky, 1982</a>). However, only approximately 42 species of decapod crustaceans are thought to be hermaphroditic, and 35 of these are caridean shrimp (see lists in <a reveal-id="R8" data-open="R8" class="link link-ref link-reveal xref-bibr">Carpenter, 1978</a>; <a reveal-id="R29" data-open="R29" class="link link-ref link-reveal xref-bibr">Policansky, 1982</a>; <a reveal-id="R3" data-open="R3" class="link link-ref link-reveal xref-bibr">Bauer, 2000</a>) (and for additional examples see <a reveal-id="R24" data-open="R24" class="link link-ref link-reveal xref-bibr">Kagwade, 1981</a>; <a reveal-id="R38" data-open="R38" class="link link-ref link-reveal xref-bibr">Sukumaran, 1981</a>; <a reveal-id="R2" data-open="R2" class="link link-ref link-reveal xref-bibr">Bauer, 1986</a>; <a reveal-id="R27" data-open="R27" class="link link-ref link-reveal xref-bibr">Nakashima, 1987</a>; <a reveal-id="R20" data-open="R20" class="link link-ref link-reveal xref-bibr">Gherardi and Calloni, 1993</a>; <a reveal-id="R19" data-open="R19" class="link link-ref link-reveal xref-bibr">Gavio <em>et al.</em>, 1994</a>; <a reveal-id="R34" data-open="R34" class="link link-ref link-reveal xref-bibr">Rudolph, 1995</a>; <a reveal-id="R12" data-open="R12" class="link link-ref link-reveal xref-bibr">de Almeida and Buckup, 1997</a>, <a reveal-id="R13" data-open="R13" class="link link-ref link-reveal xref-bibr">2000</a>; <a reveal-id="R4" data-open="R4" class="link link-ref link-reveal xref-bibr">Bauer and Holt, 1998</a>; <a reveal-id="R16" data-open="R16" class="link link-ref link-reveal xref-bibr">Fiedler, 1998</a>). Furthermore, at least two hippolytid carideans in the genus <em>Lysmata</em> display a form of simultaneous hermaphroditism (<a reveal-id="R4" data-open="R4" class="link link-ref link-reveal xref-bibr">Bauer and Holt, 1998</a>; <a reveal-id="R16" data-open="R16" class="link link-ref link-reveal xref-bibr">Fiedler, 1998</a>). Because marine decapods share many of the same ecological and habitat selection pressures with teleost fishes, it is surprising to find so few examples in this diverse group of crustaceans. Perhaps past research has not focused closely on the best crustacean candidates for sex change.</p><p>Harlequin shrimp, <em>Hymenocera picta</em> (Dana, 1852), are small caridean shrimp that may be ideal candidates for studies on plastic sex determination. They are found in shallow coastal waters (1–30 m) of the tropical Indo-Pacific region but are apparently uncommon throughout their range (personal observation; <a reveal-id="R14" data-open="R14" class="link link-ref link-reveal xref-bibr">Debelius, 1984</a>). The shrimp are hardy in captivity and are popular aquarium pets, but only eat asteroid echinoderms (<a reveal-id="R41" data-open="R41" class="link link-ref link-reveal xref-bibr">Wickler, 1973</a>; <a reveal-id="R14" data-open="R14" class="link link-ref link-reveal xref-bibr">Debelius, 1984</a>). Males and females are sexually dimorphic and form monogamous pair bonds that are maintained via mate-guarding behavior by males (<a reveal-id="R41" data-open="R41" class="link link-ref link-reveal xref-bibr">Wickler, 1973</a>; <a reveal-id="R36" data-open="R36" class="link link-ref link-reveal xref-bibr">Seibt and Wickler, 1979</a>). Both males and females are aggressive towards individuals of the same sex, and males will readily fight each other to death in an encounter. Also, adults are not known to change sex. Mature females spawn 100–5,000 eggs every 18–26 days, concurrent with their molt cycle (<a reveal-id="R36" data-open="R36" class="link link-ref link-reveal xref-bibr">Seibt and Wickler, 1979</a>; <a reveal-id="R25" data-open="R25" class="link link-ref link-reveal xref-bibr">Kraul and Nelson, 1986</a>). <em>Hymenocera</em> larvae pass through twelve planktonic zoeal stages and reach the settlement stage in 5–6 weeks (<a reveal-id="R25" data-open="R25" class="link link-ref link-reveal xref-bibr">Kraul and Nelson, 1986</a>; <a reveal-id="R15" data-open="R15" class="link link-ref link-reveal xref-bibr">Fiedler, 1994</a>). Postlarvae begin eating seastars five days after metamorphosis (<a reveal-id="R15" data-open="R15" class="link link-ref link-reveal xref-bibr">Fiedler, 1994</a>).</p><p>Given\nAddress: http://web.archive.org/web/20180603235849/https://academic.oup.com/jcb/article/22/4/750/2679743\nYou previously visited this page 37 seconds ago.\nViewport position: Showing page 16 of 45.\n=======================\nthe relative rarity of these animals, their high intraspecific aggression, the dispersal of their prey, and the low probability of larval survival, it seems likely that a mechanism ensuring access to the opposite sex would have evolved. This might be accomplished via the ability to follow distant chemical cues from the opposite sex or a mechanism of environmentally (socially) regulated juvenile sex determination. Harlequin shrimp certainly fit the criteria for one of three models for the evolution of hermaphroditism proposed by <a reveal-id="R21" data-open="R21" class="link link-ref link-reveal xref-bibr">Ghiselin (1969)</a>. The Low Density Model implies that there is an advantage in “being able to mate with any member of the species, where the probability of encountering a suitable mate is low” (<a reveal-id="R21" data-open="R21" class="link link-ref link-reveal xref-bibr">Ghiselin, 1969</a>: 200). This model suggests the evolution of simultaneous hermaphroditism or labile juvenile sex determination in marine animals, like harlequin shrimp, with limited postlarval dispersal. In this study, I assess the presence of such mechanisms in juvenile <em>Hymenocera picta</em>. These experiments examine social influence on the primary sex determination of this species.</p>                            <h2 scrollto-destination="59053183" id="59053183" class="section-title" data-legacy-id="s2">Materials and Methods</h2>\n<p>In order to examine the effect of social environment on the primary sex determination of <em>Hymenocera picta</em>, a series of five experiments was carried out in which postlarvae were placed into artificial social situations in the laboratory. In the “Paired Juveniles” experiment (replicates I, II, and III), pairs of postlarvae were isolated and observed closely over a period of several months. In the “Single Juveniles” experiment, postlarvae were isolated individually and monitored. In the “Adult/Juvenile Pairs” experiment, single postlarvae were isolated with one adult shrimp. In the “Histology/Morphology Pairs” experiment, postlarval pairs were isolated and sacrificed at regular intervals in order to examine morphological characteristics of primary sex determination. The three social contexts (juvenile conspecific, adult conspecific, and no conspecific) were intended to simulate the potential social environments that <em>Hymenocera picta</em> postlarvae experience in the field. All the experiments are explained in detail below.</p>                            <h3 scrollto-destination="59053185" id="59053185" class="section-title" data-legacy-id="s2a">Experiment One: Paired Juveniles</h3>\n<p>In this experiment, I assessed the presence of socially regulated Environmental Sex Determination (ESD) during the juvenile phase of <em>Hymenocera picta</em> by observing the behavior and differentiation of isolated juvenile pairs. Under optimal ESD, we would expect that an isolated pair of juvenile shrimp would mature as a male/female pair. Additionally, I expect that spacing behavior would reflect the sexes of the pairs as occurs in <em>H. picta</em> adults. <a reveal-id="R35" data-open="R35" class="link link-ref link-reveal xref-bibr">Seibt (1980)</a> observed artificial groupings of adult <em>H. picta</em> in the laboratory and found that individuals of the opposite sex were found in closer proximity than those of the same sex.</p><p>Three replicates of this experiment were performed and designated as replicates I, II, and III. All three replicates were conducted at the Hawaii Institute of Marine Biology under similar conditions.</p>                            <h4 scrollto-destination="59053188" id="59053188" class="section-title" data-legacy-id="s2a1">Replicate I</h4>\n<p>Forty-two <em>Hymenocera picta</em> postlarvae, approximately two weeks postmetamorphosis, were obtained from the Waikiki Aquarium. Individual shrimp were randomly selected, by drawing numbers from a hat, and placed into twenty pairs. The two extra shrimp were held in reserve and not used. Shrimp pairs were isolated in small (8 l) plastic buckets. The buckets were arranged in two wet tables and provided flowing sea water via a polyvinyl chloride (PVC) pipe frame in each wet table. Buckets had holes drilled approximately 4 cm under the rim to facilitate water flow. Each bucket was provided with its own aeration source. Shrimp were fed small (approximately 1 cm) pieces of seastar (<em>Linckia</em> spp.) <em>ad libitum</em> during the experiment. Decaying food and detritus were siphoned from the buckets as needed.</p><p>Pairs were monitored for seven months (1 July 1991–8 February 1992). The data recorded during the experiment included the Intra-Pair Distance (IPD), total length (TL), and secondary sex characteristics. These data were recorded daily during the first month of the experiment and at increasingly longer intervals (2–7 d) in the remaining six months. I measured IPD with a transparent, flexible ruler, recording the linear distance separating two shrimp between the bases of their respective antennae.
